US Grain Transportation Report

Generated: 2026-04-19 18:56 | Data through: 2026-04-14

Contents

1. Barge Freight 1.1 Barge Rates 1.2 Barge Positioning 1.3 Forward Accuracy 2. River Levels 2.1 River Conditions 2.2 River Levels & Rates 2.3 Stage Forecasts 2.4 Forecast Errors 2.5 Warning Signal 3. Harvest Timing 4. Case Study: 2022 5. Cost Indicators 6. Navigation Notices

1. Barge Freight

1.1 Barge Rates

Weekly downbound grain barge rates as a percentage of the 1976 Waterway Freight Bureau Tariff No. 7. Rates above 100% indicate spot rates exceeding the benchmark tariff.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: USDA Agricultural Marketing Service, Grain Transportation Report datasets via the Socrata Open Data API (agtransport.usda.gov). No authentication required.

Datasets: Downbound Grain Barge Rates (dataset deqi-uken, 2004–present); 1-Month Future Barge Rates (svms-9yya); Grain Transportation Cost Indicators (8uye-ieij, 2002–present, index 2017=100).

Processing: Rates are reported weekly for 7 river segments (Twin Cities, Mid-Mississippi, Lower Illinois, St. Louis, Cincinnati, Lower Ohio, Cairo-Memphis) as a percentage of the 1976 Waterway Freight Bureau Tariff No. 7. No transformations are applied beyond date parsing and type casting. St. Louis is used as the primary benchmark throughout this report.

Figure 1-1 Historical Barge Rates

Figure 1-2 Recent Barge Rates (Last 2 Years)

1.2 Barge Positioning Indicators

Proprietary indicators measuring whether barges are "in position" to meet grain transportation demand. Built from USACE lock data at Lock 27 (Chain of Rocks) and Olmsted on the Ohio River. These indicators are most predictive during harvest season (Aug-Nov) and low water conditions.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: USDA AMS / USACE Lock Performance Monitoring System data published via the AgTransport Socrata API (agtransport.usda.gov). Datasets: Loaded/Empty Barge Counts (w6ip-grsn, 2005–present) and Downbound Barge Grain Movements (n4pw-9ygw, 2003–present). USACE Notices to Navigation Interests (NTNI) via the NDC JSON API (ndc.ops.usace.army.mil). Negative barge counts (4 rows, likely accounting corrections) are excluded.

Key locks: Miss Locks 27 (Chain of Rocks, last lock on the upper Mississippi), Ohio Olmsted (captures all Ohio + Tennessee River traffic), Ark Lock 1 (Arkansas River).

Indicators:

  • Empty Return Ratio: Weekly empty-upbound barges / grain-loaded-downbound barges at Lock 27. Values <1.0 indicate fewer empties returning than grain departing.
  • Return Ratio Deviation: Current return ratio minus the historical average for that ISO week (seasonal adjustment). Computed from 2005–present.
  • Rate Spreads: Difference in barge rates between river segments (e.g., Twin Cities minus St. Louis). Computed from weekly USDA rate data joined via ISO year-week.
  • Grain Share: Grain-loaded barges as a percentage of total loaded downbound barges (grain + non-grain) at Lock 27.

Figure 1-3 Empty Return Ratio at Lock 27

Ratio of empty barges returning upriver to grain-loaded barges going downbound. Values below 1.0 indicate fewer empties returning than grain departing — a supply squeeze at loading points. Shaded regions indicate harvest months (Aug-Nov).

Figure 1-4 Return Ratio: Deviation from Seasonal Average

The key predictive signal. When the return ratio is below its seasonal norm (red bars), barges are more "out of position" than usual for that time of year. During harvest, this deviation has a 0.265 correlation with forward 4-week rate changes. During low water conditions, it strengthens to 0.318.

Figure 1-5 Rate Spreads Between River Segments

When upper-river points (Twin Cities, Illinois) are expensive relative to St. Louis, it signals demand building upriver before propagating downstream. The Twin Cities-St. Louis spread has a 0.445 correlation with forward 4-week rate changes — the strongest single indicator in our analysis.

Figure 1-6 Grain's Share of Loaded Downbound Barges

High grain share during harvest indicates peak demand pressure. Paradoxically, very high grain share tends to precede rate declines — a "peak demand passing" signal.

Figure 1-7 Lock 27 vs Olmsted (Ohio River)

Comparing empty return ratios between the Mississippi (Lock 27) and Ohio River (Olmsted). Important context: Olmsted’s ratio is structurally higher (2–3x) and cannot be interpreted the same way as Lock 27. The Ohio River is a major upbound industrial corridor — coal, aggregates, and petroleum products move upriver from the Gulf and Tennessee/Cumberland systems. Loaded upbound barges at Olmsted (16–28k/yr) vastly outnumber grain downbound (4–7k/yr), and grain accounts for only ~20–25% of loaded downbound traffic at Olmsted vs ~60% at Lock 27. A ratio of 2–3 at Olmsted is therefore normal and reflects the structural upbound-heavy traffic pattern, not a barge surplus. What matters for barge freight analysis is the relative change in Olmsted’s ratio — when it spikes or drops relative to its own baseline, it signals shifting demand across river systems.

1.3 Forward Rate Accuracy

How well do the 1-month and 3-month forward barge rate quotes predict actual realized rates? This analysis compares USDA AMS forward quotes to the rates that ultimately materialized, measuring systematic biases and identifying when the forward market is most likely to be wrong.

Data Sources & Methodology

Sources: USDA AMS Socrata API — Spot Rates (deqi-uken), 1-Month Future Rates (svms-9yya), 3-Month Future Rates (uuhv-5etw). All for St. Louis location, 2016–present.

Processing: Monthly average rates are computed for each series. The 1-month forward quote from month M is compared against the actual realized rate in month M+1; the 3-month forward from month M against the actual in month M+3. "Surprise" = actual minus forward (positive = market underestimated rates). For the harvest analysis, the August 1-month forward and July 3-month forward are compared against the average Sep-Nov realized rate.

Figure 1-8 Average Forward Surprise by Calendar Month

Shows the average difference between actual rates and what the forward market predicted, broken down by delivery month. Positive bars indicate months where the market systematically underestimates rates. October stands out with the largest average underestimation — the forward curve consistently underprices the harvest peak. May tends to overshoot (negative surprise), as the market overestimates post-planting demand. Caveat: averages are influenced by extreme years (especially 2022 for October).

Figure 1-9 Harvest Season: Forward Quotes vs Actual Rates

For each year, the July 3-month forward (purple), the August 1-month forward (orange), and the actual Sep-Nov average rate (teal) are shown side by side. This reveals whether the market adjusts its view as harvest approaches. Notable patterns: 2022 was a massive miss at both horizons; 2023-2024 the market was well-calibrated; 2016 and 2018-2019 the market overestimated harvest rates. Caveat: the "actual" is the Sep-Nov average, which smooths weekly spikes.

Figure 1-10 Forward Surprise Over Time

Monthly time series of the gap between actual and forward-quoted rates. Shaded regions mark harvest months. Notice how errors tend to cluster — runs of underestimation (positive) or overestimation (negative) persist for several months, suggesting the forward curve adjusts slowly to changing market conditions. Caveat: this is the ex-post surprise, not a tradable signal.

Figure 1-11 3-Month Forward vs Actual: Scatter Plot

Each point is a month. The dashed diagonal is perfect foresight — points above the line indicate the actual rate exceeded the 3-month-out forward (underestimation). Harvest months (red) cluster above the line, confirming the systematic harvest-season underpricing. The dispersion increases dramatically at higher rate levels, showing that the forward curve loses accuracy precisely when it matters most. Caveat: only 10 years of data; the high-rate tail is dominated by 2022.

Figure 1-12 Forward Accuracy by Rate Regime

Average forward surprise segmented by the realized rate environment: low (<300%), medium (300-500%), and high (>500% of tariff). Error bars show +/- 1 standard deviation. The forward curve is reasonably accurate in low-rate environments but dramatically underestimates rates in high-rate regimes — precisely the conditions where accurate pricing matters most for risk management. Caveat: the high-rate bucket is small (n~15) and heavily influenced by 2022.

Table 1-1 Forward Accuracy by Rate Regime

Rate RegimenHorizon Avg SurpriseMinMaxStd Dev
Low (<300%)511-Mo-12.0-121.6+66.340.7
3-Mo-33.6-213.0+94.456.7
Medium (300-500%)531-Mo-1.7-227.0+147.982.3
3-Mo+12.9-238.3+264.596.0
High (>500%)171-Mo+135.7-267.5+1298.1339.2

2. River Levels

2.1 River Conditions

USGS daily gage height and discharge data for key Mississippi River system stations. Low water conditions restrict barge drafts and tonnage; high water can halt navigation.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: USGS National Water Information System (NWIS), Daily Values Service (waterservices.usgs.gov). No authentication required.

Stations: St. Louis (07010000, discharge), Memphis (07032000, discharge), Vicksburg (07289000, discharge), Baton Rouge (07374000, gage height), Belle Chasse (07374525, gage height), Henry IL (05558300, gage height). St. Louis and Memphis report only discharge (cfs); Baton Rouge and downriver report gage height (ft).

Processing: Daily mean values (stat code 00003) are pulled for the most recent 10 years. No transformations beyond date parsing. Gage height and discharge stations are charted separately due to different units.

Figure 2-1 Historical River Levels

Figure 2-2 Recent River Levels (Last 90 Days)

2.2 River Levels & Barge Freight Rates

Data Sources & Methodology

Sources: USGS NWIS daily values (same as River Conditions above); USDA AMS barge rates (same as Barge Transportation above).

Processing: USGS daily values are averaged into weekly observations and joined to barge rates via ISO year-week matching (barge counts report Saturday, rates report Tuesday — both fall in the same ISO week). Seasonal charts show daily USGS data mapped to day-of-year (using 2020 as the reference calendar for leap year compatibility). Historical range = daily min/max across 2016–2025; historical average = daily mean across the same period. Scatter plots join weekly average levels to weekly St. Louis barge rates, colored by season (harvest Aug-Nov vs off-season).

River levels are the single most important physical driver of barge freight rates. Low water restricts barge drafts (requiring lighter loads), reduces navigable channel width, and can halt traffic entirely. The relationship is strongly non-linear: when St. Louis discharge falls into its lowest quintile, the average barge rate is 617% of tariff versus 276% in the highest quintile — a 2.2x difference concentrated entirely in the low-water tail.

Key Findings

  • Baton Rouge gage height has the strongest harvest-season correlation with rates (r = -0.46) — low stages near the Gulf directly restrict loaded barge drafts
  • St. Louis discharge is the strongest full-year indicator (r = -0.37) and the reference point for the entire upper river system
  • Memphis discharge provides the mid-river signal (r = -0.44 during harvest)
  • The relationship is regime-dependent: positioning indicators are 3-5x more predictive during low water than during normal/high water conditions

Mississippi River at St. Louis, MO

The reference gauge for Lock 27 (Chain of Rocks). Strongest overall correlation with barge rates (-0.37). In the lowest quintile of discharge, the average St. Louis barge rate is 617% of tariff vs 276% in the highest quintile.

Show: Mississippi River at St. Louis, MO vs Barge Rate Scatter Plot

Mississippi River at Baton Rouge, LA

Lower Mississippi near Gulf export terminals. Strongest harvest-season correlation with rates (-0.46). Low stages here directly restrict loaded barge drafts approaching the Gulf.

Show: Mississippi River at Baton Rouge, LA vs Barge Rate Scatter Plot

Mississippi River at Memphis, TN

Mid-Mississippi. Strong harvest correlation (-0.44). Memphis is the key mid-river waypoint; low water here creates a bottleneck between upper loading points and Gulf export terminals.

Show: Mississippi River at Memphis, TN vs Barge Rate Scatter Plot

Illinois River at Henry, IL

Illinois Waterway, the primary barge corridor for Corn Belt grain. Weaker rate correlation (-0.26) because Illinois River conditions primarily affect Illinois River barge rates rather than St. Louis.

Show: Illinois River at Henry, IL vs Barge Rate Scatter Plot

2.3 River Stage Forecasts

NWS observed and forecast river stages from the National Water Prediction Service. Dashed lines indicate forecast values. Horizontal reference lines show flood stage thresholds.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: NOAA National Water Prediction Service (NWPS) REST API (api.water.noaa.gov). No authentication required. Replaced the legacy AHPS system in March 2024.

Gauges: EADM7 (St. Louis), MEMT1 (Memphis), VCKM6 (Vicksburg), BTRL1 (Baton Rouge), NORL1 (New Orleans), HNYI2 (Henry IL), CIRI2 (Cairo IL).

Processing: The API returns ~30 days of observed data and up to 28 days of deterministic NWS RFC forecasts at 6-hour intervals. Sentinel values (-9999 for stage, -999 for flow) are replaced with NULL. Flood stage thresholds (action, minor, moderate, major) are pulled from the gauge metadata endpoint. This data is current-period only — historical forecasts are not archived by NOAA (see the Forecast Errors section for historical analysis using the IEM archive).

2.4 Forecast Errors & Surprises

How accurate are NWS river stage forecasts, and do forecast errors predict barge rate surprises? This analysis compares 10 years of NWS-issued stage forecasts (from the Iowa Environmental Mesonet HML archive) against observed stages at three key gauges.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: Iowa Environmental Mesonet (IEM), Hydrological Markup Language (HML) archive at Iowa State University (mesonet.agron.iastate.edu). This archive captures actual NWS-issued river stage forecasts and observed stages as transmitted via HML products. Data available from approximately 2014 to present.

Gauges: EADM7 (Mississippi R. at St. Louis), MEMT1 (Memphis), BTRL1 (Baton Rouge). Forecasts are issued ~2 times daily at 6-hour intervals with horizons up to 28 days.

Processing: Forecast and observed data are fetched in quarterly chunks (to respect IEM query limits) for 2016–present. Multiple intra-day issuances and 6-hourly forecast points are averaged to daily values. Forecast surprise is computed as observed_stage − forecast_stage (positive = river came in higher than forecast, negative = lower). The barge rate "surprise" uses the USDA AMS 1-month forward rate from August as the market expectation, compared against actual Sep-Nov realized rates. Regression lines in scatter plots exclude 2022 to prevent the extreme outlier from dominating the fit.

Key Findings

  • NWS forecasts have a systematic positive bias — they consistently underestimate actual river stage. At 14-day lead, St. Louis forecasts are ~5 ft too low on average.
  • Forecast accuracy degrades rapidly beyond 7 days: 7-day error is ~1.5 ft, but 28-day error is ~8.7 ft at St. Louis
  • The bias has a seasonal pattern — largest in spring (Mar-May) when rain events are hardest to predict, smallest in fall (Oct-Nov) when the river tends to follow predictable dry-season patterns
  • When river forecasts miss low — predicting the river will be higher than it turns out to be — barge rates tend to surprise to the upside

Figure 2-3 Forecast Bias by Lead Time

Average forecast error (observed minus forecast) with +/- 1 standard deviation bars. Positive values indicate the NWS underestimated the actual stage. Error grows roughly linearly with lead time.

Figure 2-4 2022 Low-Water Crisis: What Did the Forecasts Say?

Top panel: 14-day forecast (dashed orange) vs actual stage (blue) at St. Louis through the 2022 crisis. Bottom panel: daily forecast surprise. The NWS tracked the deepening low water but consistently underestimated recovery — the river came back faster than forecast in late October.

Figure 2-5 River Forecast Surprise vs Barge Rate Surprise

Monthly scatter for Aug-Nov across all years. The x-axis shows the average 14-day stage forecast surprise (negative = river came in lower than expected). The y-axis shows the barge rate surprise (actual rate minus the August 1-month forward quote). When the NWS underestimates how low the river will go, barge rates tend to surprise to the upside.

Figure 2-6 Seasonal Pattern of Forecast Bias

Average 14-day forecast error by month and gauge. The spring bias (Mar-May) reflects the difficulty of predicting rain-driven rises. The fall dip (Sep-Nov) reflects more predictable base flow conditions.

2.5 River Level Warning Signal

A composite signal derived from Mississippi River discharge levels, momentum, and multi-gauge confirmation. Based on backtested analysis of 10 years of weekly data.

Data Sources & Methodology

Sources: USGS Water Services API (waterservices.usgs.gov) for daily discharge and gage height; NOAA National Water Prediction Service API (api.water.noaa.gov) for current river stage forecasts and flood thresholds.

Processing: Daily discharge values are averaged into weekly observations and aligned to barge rate reporting weeks via ISO year-week matching. The 4-week percent change in St. Louis discharge (USGS site 07010000) forms the core momentum signal. Signal states (ALERT, WATCH, FAVORABLE, NEUTRAL) are defined by thresholds derived from quintile analysis of the historical relationship between discharge level + momentum and forward 4-week barge rate changes. All thresholds were backtested on 2016–2025 data.

NEUTRAL
Neutral: Normal conditions, no strong directional signal.
STL Discharge: 330,000 cfs 4-Week Change: +84.2% All Gauges Falling: No
ALERT: Low water (<150k cfs) and falling (>10% decline over 4 weeks). Historically: avg +63 rate points over next 4 weeks (n=113). During harvest: avg +101 rate points.
WATCH: Discharge falling rapidly (>27% decline) or approaching critical threshold (<200k cfs and declining >10%). Threshold crossing below 150k cfs historically preceded avg +65 rate points.
FAVORABLE: Low water but recovering (>10% rise). Recovery from low water historically preceded avg -114 rate points (rate decline).
NEUTRAL: Normal conditions, no strong directional signal from river levels.

Figure 2-7 Signal History with Barge Rates

Top panel shows St. Louis barge rates colored by signal state. Bottom panel shows discharge with the 150,000 cfs critical threshold.

Figure 2-8 Discharge Momentum

The 4-week rate of change in St. Louis discharge is the core signal driver. Red bars indicate falling discharge; green indicates rising. The -10% and -27% thresholds mark the Watch and Alert trigger levels.

Table 2-1 NOAA Forecast Direction

Current NWS forecast vs recent observed averages. Rising forecasts suggest easing rate pressure; falling forecasts suggest tightening. Note: forecast data cannot be backtested (only current period available), but provides valuable forward-looking context.

GaugeObserved AvgForecast AvgDirection
Baton Rouge19.218.1▼ -6%
Cairo IL27.428.1▲ +3%
St. Louis14.024.7▲ +77%
Henry IL18.722.4▲ +20%
Memphis12.613.1▲ +4%
New Orleans6.15.4▼ -12%
Vicksburg25.324.8▼ -2%

3. Harvest Timing

3.1 Harvest Timing & Barge Freight Rates

Harvest timing and compression directly drive barge demand. When harvest is compressed into fewer weeks, more bushels compete for limited barge capacity simultaneously, pushing rates higher. This analysis converts USDA weekly harvest progress into actual bushels harvested per week using state-level grain production estimates for 10 key barge states (IL, IA, IN, MN, MO, OH, WI, KY, SD, NE).

Data Sources & Methodology

Sources: USDA NASS QuickStats API (quickstats.nass.usda.gov) for weekly crop progress (PCT HARVESTED) and annual production (bushels). API key required (free registration).

States: IL, IA, IN, MN, MO, OH, WI, KY, SD, NE — the 10 states that feed the majority of grain volume onto the Mississippi River barge system.

Production: Corn uses util_practice_desc = 'GRAIN' to exclude silage. Soybeans uses 'ALL UTILIZATION PRACTICES'. Final reference_period_desc = 'YEAR' estimates are used.

Progress deduplication: Some states (IN, OH, MN, WI) report duplicate progress rows per week (one for grain corn, one for all corn including silage). The minimum value is taken per state/week to select the grain-specific series.

Daily interpolation: Weekly NASS progress reports are interpolated to daily values. For normal weeks (gaps ≤ 14 days), linear interpolation is used between reported cumulative percentages. For the first reported week, daily values ramp linearly from 0% over the prior 7 days. If the final report is below 100%, a synthetic 100% point is added 7 days later. For large data gaps (>14 days, primarily 2025), the seasonal-average daily pace from complete years (2016–2024) is used to distribute the gap’s progress, scaled to match the known start and end percentages.

Weekly bushels: Daily incremental harvest percentage × state grain production, summed across states and aggregated into ISO weeks. Each state’s cumulative harvest is capped at 100% to prevent overstatement.

Seasonal charts: Built from daily data with a 7-day centered rolling average applied to each year’s trace for smoothness.

N-S Spread index: States are classified as North (IA, MN, WI, SD, NE) or South (MO, KY, IN, OH) of St. Louis. The spread is the difference in average day-of-year when each group reaches 50% corn harvested, computed from daily-interpolated progress for day-level precision.

Key Findings

  • Weekly bushels harvested has a +0.22 correlation with concurrent barge rate levels — more grain moving = higher rates, as expected
  • Shorter harvest windows (weeks from 25% to 75% complete) are associated with higher average harvest-season rates (r = -0.30 for corn)
  • Total production volume correlates negatively with rates (r = -0.34 for corn) — big crop years tend to have more efficient logistics and lower rates, except when combined with other stress factors (2022)
  • 2022 stands out: a compressed 6-week corn harvest (25-75%) coinciding with historic low water produced the most extreme rate environment on record

Figure 3-1 Corn: Daily Bushels Harvested (7-Day Avg)

Weekly grain corn bushels harvested across 10 barge states. Click years in the legend to compare harvest timing across years. 2022 is highlighted by default.

Figure 3-2 Soybeans: Daily Bushels Harvested (7-Day Avg)

Soybean harvest typically starts 1-2 weeks before corn and finishes slightly earlier.

Figure 3-3 Combined Corn + Soybeans: Daily Grain Volume (7-Day Avg)

The combined weekly bushels harvested represents the total grain volume competing for barge capacity. Peak weeks can see over 3 billion bushels harvested across these states.

Table 3-1 Harvest Compression Metrics

Key metrics by crop and year. "Weeks 25-75%" measures how compressed the core harvest window is — fewer weeks means more intense demand pressure on barges.

Figure 3-4 North-South Harvest Spread vs Barge Rates

The number of days between when southern river states (MO, KY, IN, OH) and northern states (IA, MN, WI, SD, NE) reach 50% corn harvested. A wider spread means harvest demand rolls through the river system in waves rather than hitting all at once. The dashed orange line shows the August 1-month forward rate — what the market expected heading into harvest.

Figure 3-5 N-S Spread vs Harvest Rate: Scatter Plot

Each point is a year. The regression line and statistics exclude 2022 (red) since the low water crisis overwhelmed the harvest timing effect. The negative relationship shows that more staggered harvests tend to produce lower barge rates.

Figure 3-6 N-S Spread vs Rate Surprise

"Surprise" is the difference between the actual Sep-Nov average barge rate and what the market was pricing in August (1-month forward). Positive = market underestimated harvest-season rates. A compressed N-S spread should lead to larger positive surprises since the market may not fully anticipate the demand compression.

Annual harvest timing indices for corn alongside St. Louis barge rates. "Aug 1-Mo Fwd" is the average August forward rate quote (market expectation for Sep). "Surprise" is the difference between the actual harvest average and the August forward — positive means the market underestimated harvest-season rates.

4. Case Study: 2022 Mississippi River Low-Water Crisis

The 2022 low-water event produced the most extreme barge rate spike in modern history. St. Louis rates surged from 412% of tariff in late August to a peak of 2,653% on October 11 — a 6.4x increase in six weeks. This section examines the contributing factors.

Data Sources & Methodology

Sources: All data in this section is drawn from the same sources described above — USDA AMS barge rates, USGS discharge data, USACE barge counts, and USDA cost indicators — filtered to calendar year 2022. The dual-axis rate vs discharge chart joins weekly St. Louis barge rates to daily USGS discharge at site 07010000. Monthly discharge normals are computed as the average across 2016–2025 (excluding 2022) for comparison.

Figure 4-1 Rate Spike vs River Discharge

St. Louis discharge fell to 61% below normal in October 2022, the worst month. The river had been below normal all year (partly driven by La Nina drought conditions), but the deficit accelerated from July onward just as harvest began.

Figure 4-2 Barge Positioning Collapse

The return ratio at Lock 27 tells a dramatic story. In September, it spiked to 2.03 as empty barges returned but there was little grain to load (tonnage collapsed 70%). Then in October, it crashed to 0.29 — the most extreme barge shortage in the dataset — as export demand surged but low water made full loading impossible.

Figure 4-3 Multi-Modal Cost Divergence

The barge cost index hit 605 (vs 2017=100 base) in October while truck (~197) and rail (~158) saw much smaller increases. With barges unable to carry full loads, grain was forced onto trucks and rail at premium costs. The Russia-Ukraine war (Feb 2022) had already elevated global grain demand, and Class I railroad service problems further constrained alternatives.

Figure 4-4 Historical Context

The 2022 peak of 2,653% dwarfed all previous extremes. The second-highest peak on record was 1,150% in 2008 (also a drought year). The event demonstrated how multiple factors — persistent drought, harvest timing, global trade disruption, and rail congestion — can compound to create non-linear price spikes in barge freight.

5. Transportation Cost Indicators

USDA multi-modal grain transportation cost index (2017 = 100). Compares relative cost trends across truck, rail (shuttle train), barge, and ocean vessel modes.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: USDA AMS Grain Transportation Report, Cost Indicators dataset (8uye-ieij) via Socrata API (agtransport.usda.gov). Published weekly, 2002–present.

Processing: Index values (base 2017 = 100) for truck, shuttle train, barge, Gulf vessel, and Pacific vessel are plotted directly from the source data with no transformations beyond date parsing.

Figure 5-1 Multi-Modal Cost Indicators

6. Active Navigation Notices

Current USACE Notices to Navigation Interests for grain-relevant waterway districts. These may include lock closures, channel restrictions, or other navigation hazards.

Data Sources & Methodology

Source: USACE Navigation Data Center, Notices to Navigation Interests (NTNI) JSON API (ndc.ops.usace.army.mil). Queried by district code.

Districts: MVS (St. Louis — Lock 25, Lock 27), MVR (Rock Island — LaGrange Lock, upper MS), MVN (New Orleans — IHNC Lock), LRL (Louisville — Ohio River), MVM (Memphis).

Processing: Active notices are fetched at report generation time. Notice body text is truncated to 500 characters. This data is current-period only (not archived).
